question_title: Will CDC report cumulative influenza-associated pediatric deaths of at least 140 for the 2025–26 U.S. flu season before April 30, 2026?
summary:
The question asks whether CDC's cumulative pediatric flu death count for 2025-26 will reach 140 by the last FluView report published on or before April 30, 2026. Currently at 101 deaths (Week 9 report, published March 13), reaching 140 requires 39 more deaths over approximately 6 remaining FluView reports (through ~Week 15, published ~April 24).

The recent reporting rate of 11 deaths/week (weeks 8-9) significantly exceeds the needed average of 6.5/week. Even with moderate weekly decline (~10-15%), the cumulative total should surpass 140. Starting at 11/week, the decline rate would need to exceed ~22% per week to miss the threshold - unusually fast for death reporting given its inherent lag. However, if the recent 11/week rate partly reflects backlog clearing and the underlying rate is closer to 7-8/week, the margin narrows considerably.

Monte Carlo simulations with starting rate ~9.5/week and ~10%/week decline give P(≥140) ≈ 64%. The season is classified as high severity for children with the second-highest hospitalization rate since 2010-11, and flu B increasing in some regions could sustain tail activity. On balance, YES is moderately favored but not certain, as the threshold sits near the projected median outcome.

factors.description: Current reporting rate of 11 pediatric deaths/week (weeks 8-9) is well above the 6.5/week average needed to reach 140 over 6 remaining reports. Starting at 11/week, decline would need to exceed 22%/week to fail.
Flu activity is clearly declining - test positivity fell from ~29% peak to 15.3%, hospitalizations declining 15% week-over-week, healthcare visits declining. Deaths will follow with lag.
Death reporting has a long tail due to delayed reports. In 2024-25, 10 deaths/week were still being reported at week 18 (May) despite low activity. This lag sustains cumulative count growth.

Season classified as 'high severity' for children with second-highest cumulative hospitalization rate since 2010-11. Low vaccine effectiveness (38-41% for children). Influenza B increasing in some regions could sustain tail activity.
Only 6 remaining FluView reports before April 30 deadline. Week 16 report likely published May 1 (Friday), after the Thursday April 30 deadline.
Monte Carlo simulation with starting rate N(9.5,2.5) and weekly decline N(10%,6%) over 6 reports gives P(>=140) = 64.4%, median final total of 145.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.65 (65% probability YES)**

Key findings:
- **Current status**: 101 cumulative pediatric deaths as of Week 9 (March 7, 2026)
- **Gap**: 39 more deaths needed over ~6 remaining FluView reports
- **Recent rate**: 11/week (last 2 weeks), well above the 6.5/week average needed
- **Declining season**: Flu positivity at 15.3% and falling, but death reporting lags activity decline by 2-4 weeks
- **Threshold analysis**: Starting at 11/week, death reporting decline would need to exceed 22%/week to miss 140 — unusually steep. Even at a more conservative 8-9/week start, decline needs to exceed 10-14%/week to miss.
- **Simulation**: Monte Carlo gives ~64% probability with median final total of ~145

The main uncertainty is whether the recent 11/week reporting rate is sustained or was a temporary spike from backlog clearing. The threshold sits near the projected median, making this a genuinely uncertain question that tilts moderately toward YES.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the last FluView report published on or before April 30, 2026 at https://www.cdc.gov/fluview/index.html reports cumulative influenza-associated pediatric deaths for the 2025–26 season of 140 or more before April 30, 2026. Otherwise, it resolves to no.
fine_print: Use the latest FluView published on or before the deadline. All FluView figures are preliminary and may be revised later; use the value shown in that last eligible FluView. Candidate Sources: CDC FluView main page at https://www.cdc.gov/fluview/index.html ; CDC FluView Interactive linked from that page.
